one_line_takeaway: Instead of telling an LLM to ignore injected instructions, make it self-report which line's instruction it executed, then filter to keep only responses referencing the original instruction — driving prompt-injection ASR to near 0% with almost no utility cost.
key_finding: The referencing+filtering defense drives ASR to near 0% while barely affecting utility, and generalizes to unseen attacks better than the fine-tuned StruQ baseline. On direct injection (AlpacaFarm) it beats every prompt-engineering baseline by at least 19.71% across all attacks/models, with per-cell ASR as low as 0.00% (Llama3-8B under Ignore/Fakecom). On indirect injection its maximum ASR is only 4.00% on Inj-SQuAD and 7.00% on Inj-TriviaQA, versus a best-baseline ASR of 19.67% on Inj-SQuAD. It holds on 70B-405B models (max ASR 2.78%) and closed models (GPT-4o-mini down to 0.22%; GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 average <1%). Crucially, StruQ generalizes poorly to unknown attacks like Fakecom/Combined (e.g. Qwen2-7B Inj-SQuAD Fakecom 78.56%, Combined 82.78%), whereas this method stays low. Utility is essentially preserved (QA sometimes improved; SST2 average drop only 1.53%, vs. StruQ's 5.77% average accuracy loss).
core_contribution: A training-free, prompt-engineering defense against prompt injection called 'Robustness via Referencing.' Rather than suppressing an LLM's tendency to follow injected instructions (the dominant prior strategy, e.g. Sandwich/Reminder/Spotlight and the fine-tuned StruQ), the method leverages instruction-following: the defended prompt splits data content into tagged lines ('[L X]'), separates instruction vs. data areas with '<Instruction Area>'/'<Data Area>' delimiters, and instructs the model to emit, for each executed instruction, a tuple of (line tag, referenced instruction, response) terminated by an '[end]' token, aided by two in-context examples. A downstream filter keeps only the tuple whose tag equals the original instruction's tag (always '[L 1]', since the original instruction is placed first) and discards all others, yielding a clean output. The motivating observation (from the authors' own analysis of successful attacks) is that a compromised LLM often still correctly references which instruction it is executing (e.g. 'For the second instruction...'), so that reference can be exploited to filter out injected-instruction responses. The paper also proposes a dynamic-reference-tag variant (tag randomly drawn from a vocabulary per interaction) to blunt adaptive attacks that would otherwise spoof a static tag.
threat_model: Direct and indirect prompt injection. Attacker's goal is goal hijacking: making the LLM's response contain an answer to an injected instruction. Attacker accessibility is limited to the data content only, and cannot modify the system prompt, model parameters, or other system components (in direct injection the app developer fixes the original instruction and all user/attacker input is treated as data; in indirect injection the malicious instruction is planted in external content such as web pages retrieved by tools). Knowledge assumptions: for prompt-engineering attacks the attacker knows nothing about the deployed model, system prompt, or defense (so adaptive attacks are treated as impractical); for gradient-based attacks the attacker is assumed to know the model to compute gradients. The authors acknowledge a residual adaptive-attack risk if the static reference tag leaks, which they mitigate (but do not eliminate) with dynamic tags.
limitations: Relies on carefully engineered prompts plus ICL example outputs to make models produce the structured tagged format; ablation shows removing the ICL examples causes some models (Qwen2-7B on TriviaQA/SST, Llama3.1-8B on TriviaQA) to lose utility because correct answers get filtered out when structure breaks. Being prompt-engineering-based, it admits no formal/mathematical guarantee (a limitation the authors note is shared by prior defenses). Adaptive attacks that learn the reference tag can defeat the static version; the dynamic-tag mitigation still has a non-zero (though low) probability of the attacker-chosen tag matching the drawn tag. Gradient-attack ASR, while lower than StruQ, is not near-zero (Qwen2-7B: 6.25% GCG, 8.65% AutoDAN). The static-tag assumption of stealth is an explicit modeling choice rather than a proven property.

datasets_used: Inj-SQuAD (indirect injection, 900 samples; from Chen et al. 2025b, built on SQuAD)
Inj-TriviaQA (indirect injection, 900 samples; from Chen et al. 2025b, built on TriviaQA)
SQuAD, TriviaQA, SST2 (utility evaluation, no attack)
quantitative_results: Direct injection (AlpacaFarm): the method beats all prompt-engineering baselines by at least 19.71% across all attacks/models; per-cell ASR includes Llama3-8B Naive 2.88%, Ignore 0.00%, Fakecom 0.00%, Combined 0.96%
Indirect Inj-SQuAD: maximum ASR of the method is 4.00% vs. best-baseline lowest ASR of 19.67% (Sandwich); e.g. Llama3.1-8B under the method 0.11-0.33% across attacks
Indirect Inj-TriviaQA: maximum ASR of the method is 7.00%
Larger models (70B-405B, Inj-SQuAD): maximum ASR 2.78% (Qwen2-72B Naive)
Closed-source (Inj-SQuAD): GPT-4o-mini as low as 0.22% across all five attacks; GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 average ASR below 1%
Gradient attacks (Qwen2-7B, direct): method 6.25% GCG / 8.65% AutoDAN vs. StruQ 10.10% / 14.42%; Llama3-8B method 3.85% GCG / 6.25% AutoDAN
Utility (Table 3): method preserves or improves QA accuracy (e.g. Llama3-8B SQuAD 87.78% vs. None 83.56%); SST2 average drop only 1.53%; StruQ reduces average accuracy by 5.77%
StruQ generalization failure on unseen attacks: Qwen2-7B Inj-SQuAD Fakecom 78.56%, Combined 82.78% ASR (vs. method ~1.4-1.8%)
